canonical_definition: Stanford Dogs is consistently defined as a dataset for fine-grained visual classification, specifically for identifying 120 different breeds of dogs. It is explicitly used as a measurement instrument to assess the construct of "Fine-grained understanding". The dataset is situated as one of several within the larger FGVC benchmark, which focuses on Fine-Grained Visual Classification tasks. While its common application appears to be for classification, its use can be adapted. For instance, one paper notes that they "reformulate the task as a 4-way multiple-choice question," indicating some variability in how the benchmark is operationalized in practice.
papers: NESTFUL: A Benchmark for EvaluatingLLMs on Nested Sequences ofAPICalls
ProtoVQA: An Adaptable Prototypical Framework for Explainable Fine-Grained Visual Question Answering
Enhancing Cognition and Explainability of Multimodal Foundation Models with Self-Synthesized Data
Revisiting Prefix-tuning: Statistical Benefits of Reparameterization among Prompts

definitions.text: A dataset within the FGVC benchmark for the fine-grained visual classification of 120 breeds of dogs.
